title: Silver cleaning paste
subtitle: A gentle abrasive paste of bicarbonate of soda, cream of tartar, salt, and castile soap for silver
excerpt: This paste cleans silver through a mild chemical reaction as well as gentle mechanical action. Bicarbonate of soda is alkaline, cream of tartar is acidic, and together with salt they form a mild cleaning compound that removes tarnish from silver without scratching. Always rinse thoroughly.
sourceNotes: Silver cleaning chemistry from standard household chemistry references and Royal Society of Chemistry guides.

recipe.batchNotes: Make in the quantity needed. This paste keeps well in a sealed jar.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for 3 months in a sealed jar.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 3 months in a sealed jar. May dry out slightly; add a few drops of water and stir to restore consistency.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dark layer of silver sulphide (Ag2S) that forms on the surface of silver objects when exposed to sulphur compounds in the air. It is a surface reaction only and does not damage the underlying silver.

Potassium bitartrate (KHC4H4O6), a mildly acidic powder that is a by-product of winemaking. In cleaning pastes it provides mild acidity to break down alkaline deposits and helps the formula act on the tarnish layer.
Sodium bicarbonate. A gentle abrasive and mild alkali used in cleaning pastes to lift surface grime and tarnish.

A silver cleaning method in which an electrochemical reaction transfers the tarnish (silver sulphide) back to aluminium foil via a saline solution. The paste method in this recipe uses mild chemical and mechanical action rather than electrochemical transfer.

content.text: is silver sulphide, a surface layer that forms in air. This paste removes it through a combination of mild chemical action and gentle abrasion.
provides mild acidity, and castile soap holds the formula together and lifts residue. Note that this method is distinct from
, which uses aluminium foil and salt water. Rinse silver thoroughly after use to remove all paste residue. Shelf life is 3 months.

Combine bicarbonate of soda, cream of tartar, and salt in the mixing bowl and stir. Add the liquid castile soap and mix until a stiff paste forms. The mixture should hold together without crumbling. Transfer to the jar.

To use: apply a small amount of paste to a soft cloth and rub gently onto the silver surface using circular motions. Rinse thoroughly under running water. Dry immediately with a clean cloth. Do not use on silver plate, as repeated abrasion can wear through the plating. Do not use on oxidised (intentionally darkened) silver jewellery.
Store in a sealed jar for up to 3 months. If the paste dries out, add a few drops of water and stir to restore. Discard if the paste smells sour or unusual.
